polytechnic.text: Final-year software engineering student focused on building reliable software systems and applying strong technical foundations in programming, system design, and teamwork.

hec.text: Completed an entrepreneurship-focused certification to strengthen product strategy, commercialization, and business thinking for technology-driven projects.

desjardins.title: Cybersecurity Intern
desjardins.text: Supported external perimeter surveillance for one of Quebec's largest banks, working across more than 7K web assets. Collaborated with SOC teams and used SIEM and API protection tooling, while improving practical skills in threat intelligence, risk management, and compliance.

trydon.title: Co-Founder & CEO
trydon.text: Built a virtual try-on and return reduction solution using web analytics and body-recognition AI, reaching about 90% measurement precision. Led a cross-functional team delivering web, mobile, and Shopify app experiences, with early user traction.

polystar.title: President
polystar.text: Coordinate robotics projects and organization logistics for a community of 50+ members. Direct multidisciplinary teams in design, coding, and competition strategy, while maintaining a strong execution culture.

desjungles.title: Desjungles - Accessibility & safety focused mobile app
desjungles.description: Developed a cross-platform mobile application using React Native. We used Face recognition AI, environnement detection (light, movement) and a combination of speech command with text to speech to make transaction without touchscreen interaction.
desjungles.section1Text1: Desjungles is the result of my team’s participation in the Hackathon at Concordia University's ConuHacks X in January 2026. This Hackathon is divided into multiple challenges sponsored by different companies. As part of the more than 1,000 participants in the 2026 edition, we decided to participate in the Desjardins Challenge, for which we won first place. This challenge consisted of making a Banking App focused on accessibility, using adventure as a storyline, the app should be usable without hands and even without seeing the screen, adapt to its environment and still be able to do basic banking operations.
desjungles.section2Text1: Our application is a banking app, where the user can transfer money from one of their bank accounts to another, and can send money to someone else. This is done while adapting to the user's environment, while showing our creativity. The following are the core features of our application:
desjungles.section2Bullet1: The phone is using face recognation to detect how many person are looking at the screen, if this number is different from 1, the page goes into secret mode.
desjungles.section2Bullet2: Secret mode is a text-to-speech banking service, the user can take action by saying a series of accepted transactions, and each action will be confirmed by the app using text-to-speech.
desjungles.section2Bullet3: The ambient light in the user's environment will dictate the theme mode of the user's application.
desjungles.section2Bullet4: When the user is in motion, they will be immersed in a jungle-like environment. Furthermore, the application will show the Home screen while disabling any other navigation buttons, ensuring security.
desjungles.section3Text1: When the user is in motion, they will be immersed in a jungle-like environment. Furthermore, the application will show the Home screen while disabling any other navigation buttons, ensuring security.
desjungles.section3Text2: Using Expo libraries we were able to use the gyroscope and detect movement. For the light sensor, we were able to use the natively provided one on Android. This sensor wasn’t available on Android so we used exposure on pictures taken every 1.5 seconds by the face camera.
desjungles.section3Text3: To detect the amount of faces  we used the model BlazeFace, this model is made to recognize faces but we only used it to count the amount detected. When the amount is 0 or 2 we use context to activate secret mode
desjungles.section3Text4: We used … to transform the user speech intro text. Since we cannot assure the user is in a calm place, we parse the whole text and only keep the part between 2 tags « Captain ». We then use a series of regex like the follozinf to extract a transaction command.
desjungles.section3Text5: After a command is understand, the request is sent to the server the transaction is done and a feedback is told to the user with the text-to-speech expo module.
desjungles.section4Text1: As part of the team, I participated in the architecture conception at the beginning of the Hackathon. For the rest of the competition, my task was managing the general app architecture, building the database using mongo. I chose and integrated TensorFlow and the face recognition model. Finally, I built the voice command and text to speech system.

trydon.title: Trydon - AI powered size recommendation for online fashion
trydon.description: Trydon is a technology that analyzes body measurements and garment specifications to determine how clothing will fit before buying online.
trydon.context: Startup project - Polytechnique Montréal & HEC Montréal incubators
trydon.section1Text1: Buying clothes online is convenient but sizing remains one of the biggest frustrations for customers. Each brand uses different size charts and garments are often described only by simple labels such as S, M or L. Trydon was created to solve this problem by allowing shoppers to rely on their real body measurements instead of generic size labels.
trydon.section2Text1: Trydon helps shoppers determine the best size before buying clothing online. By comparing the user's body measurements with the garment's technical measurements, the system estimates how the item will fit and recommends the most appropriate size.
trydon.section2Bullet1: Users create a profile, get measured using a body segmentation AI model, and store their measurements securely in their account.
trydon.section2Bullet2: Garment measurements are extracted from brand tech packs or product data.
trydon.section2Bullet3: A fit algorithm compares body and garment measurements to estimate tightness, looseness and comfort.
trydon.section2Bullet4: The result is displayed directly on e-commerce websites through a Shopify app integration.
trydon.section3Text1: The core of Trydon is a measurement-based fit algorithm. Instead of predicting a size purely from historical purchases or machine learning models, we rely on real geometric comparisons between body dimensions and garment dimensions.
trydon.section3Text2: Users measurements are taken using the segmentation AI model BodyPix taht we added to the plugin using TensorFlow. When browsing an online store, the Trydon system retrieves the garment measurements and calculates differences across key dimensions such as chest, waist, hips and inseam.
trydon.section3Text3: These measurements are processed by our fit scoring algorithm which determines whether the garment will be tight, fitted or loose depending on the margin between body and garment measurements.
trydon.section3Text4: The final result is presented as a simple size recommendation along with a visual explanation of how the garment will fit.

diajiro.title: Dia Jiro - Sound controlled cave exploration game
diajiro.description: A Unity GameJam project where players use their voice to activate crystals based on sound frequencies.
diajiro.section1Text1: Dia Jiro was created during a GameJam where one of the main constraints was to use a maximum of five colors in the entire game. Our idea was to design a cave exploration game centered around mysterious crystals, each represented by one of the allowed colors. Instead of traditional controls, we introduced a unique mechanic where players interact with the environment using their voice.
diajiro.section2Text1: The player explores a dark cave filled with magical crystals. Each crystal corresponds to a specific sound frequency. By producing sounds through the computer microphone, players activate different crystals and unlock new paths inside the cave.
diajiro.section2Bullet1: The game features five crystal types, each associated with a unique color.
diajiro.section2Bullet2: Each color corresponds to a specific sound frequency range.
diajiro.section2Bullet3: Players must experiment with different tones to activate the correct crystal.
diajiro.section2Bullet4: Lighting effects guide the player through the cave as crystals illuminate the environment.
diajiro.section3Text1: The game uses the computer's microphone as an input device. Instead of recognizing speech, we analyze the sound spectrum to detect the dominant frequency produced by the player.
diajiro.section3Text2: Using Unity's audio input system, we capture real-time audio data and compute a frequency spectrum using Fast Fourier Transform (FFT). This allows us to determine which frequency is currently dominant in the player's sound.
diajiro.section3Text3: Each crystal is associated with a specific frequency range. When the detected frequency falls within that range, the corresponding crystal lights up and triggers game events such as opening a path or illuminating the cave.
diajiro.section3Text4: This mechanic encourages players to experiment with different tones, whistles, or vocal sounds to solve puzzles and explore the cave.
diajiro.section3Text5: The player can move in every direction using traditional controls, some plateform move using sound and we made the experience more confortable by adding a virtual speaker after the first enigma, to avoid using your voice for too long and give the player a break.
diajiro.section4Bullet2: C# for gameplay and audio processing
diajiro.section4Bullet3: Microphone audio input for real-time interaction
diajiro.section4Bullet4: FFT frequency analysis for sound detection
diajiro.section5Text1: During the GameJam, I worked on the core audio interaction system. I implemented the microphone input and the frequency detection system that maps player sounds to crystal activation. I also contributed to the gameplay design to ensure the puzzles based on sound frequencies were intuitive and fun to experiment with.

polygames.title: Dia Jiro - sound-based video game enigma
polygames.description: Built and presented a Unity (C#) prototype under time constraints, winning the technical challenge category. This video game uses sound input frequency to light up differents paths and enigmas in a cave escape game.

trydonAlgo.title: Trydon - Body Measurement & Size Recommendation System
trydonAlgo.description: Designed and implemented a body measurement and size recommendation system for my company, Trydon. We achievied approximately 90% measurement precision using computer vision and machine learning techniques. This system is paired with a web plugin to showcase grament fitting recommendations and a Shopify app to provide size recommendations on product pages.

obelisk.title: Obelisk - Calendar to Timesheet AI tool
obelisk.description: Developed an AI-powered calendar to timesheet tool that automatically generates timesheet entries from calendar events. We built a web app using React and the classification in a NestJS backend. Connected to it, we have a python module using a 2 Tower AI model implementation to link calendar event with timesheet tasks
obelisk.heroSubtitle: Automating employee timesheets by intelligently linking Google Calendar events to Wrike tasks using a custom-built two-tower machine learning system.
obelisk.section1Text1: Filling out timesheets is one of the most tedious recurring tasks for software teams. Employees already describe their work inside calendar events, yet that information never flows automatically into project management tools. Obelisk was born from this gap: a final-year capstone project built in collaboration with a client company, designed to close the loop between what people schedule and what they report.
obelisk.section1Text2: The client wanted an autonomous classification pipeline that could match calendar events to the correct Wrike tasks with minimal manual intervention, while continuously improving from user corrections over time. All development was done under a non-disclosure agreement, so the client's identity remains confidential.
obelisk.section2Text1: Obelisk reads a user's Google Calendar, surfaces every event alongside the Wrike task Obelisk believes it maps to, and lets the user confirm or correct each link. Confirmed pairs are committed as timesheet entries on Wrike automatically.
obelisk.section2Bullet1: Events are fetched from Google Calendar and displayed in a clean review interface where each one is paired with a Wrike task prediction and a confidence indicator.
obelisk.section2Bullet2: The user can accept suggestions in bulk or override individual links before submitting — all corrections feed back into the model to improve future predictions.
obelisk.section2Bullet3: Once validated, the backend calls the Wrike API to create timesheet entries, removing all manual data entry from the process.
obelisk.section3Text1: Obelisk is made of three independent services that communicate over a well-defined API contract: a React web app for the user interface, a NestJS backend for orchestration and data management, and a Python server hosting the machine learning pipeline.
obelisk.section3Text2: The frontend is built with React and tailwind for a liquid-glass design system: frosted-glass cards, layered translucency, and smooth blur effects to give the interface a refined, depth-rich feel. The main view presents a calendar you can toggle from google events to wrike tasks, with visual confidence cues so users know at a glance how certain the model is about each match.
obelisk.section3Text3: The NestJS server acts as the central orchestrator. It handles OAuth flows for Google Calendar and Wrike, wraps both third-party APIs, and exposes clean REST endpoints to the frontend. All data — users, events, tasks, predictions, and correction history — is persisted in PostgreSQL through TypeORM. The classification endpoint accepts an event payload and try to classify it using simple ruling, if that fails it routes the event to the Python ML server. Finally, if the ML didn't find a match with high confidence, the backend falls back to a LLM using lightLLM
obelisk.section3Text4: The core of Obelisk is a two-tower architecture designed to capture two complementary signals. A semantic tower leverages an existing pre-trained sentence embedding model (SEMMODEL) to encode the natural-language meaning of event titles and descriptions. A structured tower is trained from scratch to encode categorical and numerical features: time of day, duration, recurrence patterns, assigned teammates, and calendar metadata.
obelisk.section3Text5: Both tower outputs are concatenated and passed through a fusion head — a shallow network that learns to weight semantic versus structured signals depending on the task. The structured tower and the fusion head are retrained incrementally through a feedback loop: each user correction becomes a new labeled training sample, allowing the model to adapt to each team's vocabulary and scheduling patterns over time.
obelisk.section3Text6: This design was chosen deliberately: freezing the semantic tower keeps training fast and avoids catastrophic forgetting on language understanding, while the trainable towers give the system the flexibility to learn organisation-specific patterns that a generic language model would never capture.
obelisk.section4Text1: As part of this team project, i had the opportunity to use my leadership and contribute in multiple areas across the stack.
obelisk.section4Bullet1: ML pipeline — Conceived the two-tower architecture from scratch: designed the feature spaces for both towers, implemented the structured tower and the fusion head in Python, wired the feedback loop for incremental retraining, and validated the model against held-out correction data.
obelisk.section4Bullet2: Classification endpoint — Built the NestJS server along with its rule based classification layer and OAuth 2.0 flows for Google and Wrike. I also implemented the fallback to lightLLM when the ML model doesn't have a high confidence prediction.
obelisk.section4Bullet3: Database — Designed the PostgreSQL schema with TypeORM: entities for users, calendar events, Wrike tasks, prediction records, and correction history, with the right indexes and relations to support the feedback loop efficiently.
obelisk.section4Bullet4: Frontend — Rebuilt the React interface with a liquid-glass design system: frosted-glass card components, layered blur and transparency effects, and a layout that makes the event-to-task review flow feel effortless.

trydonWebsite.title: Trydon.ca - Company website & customer platform
trydonWebsite.description: Designed and built the full public-facing website for Trydon, the company I co-founded. Includes marketing sections, a live ROI calculator, pricing plans, a contact and meeting scheduler, and a full account management system.
trydonWebsite.heroSubtitle: The full public website for Trydon — marketing, pricing, ROI calculator, meeting scheduler, and account management, built and deployed solo.
trydonWebsite.section1Text1: Once Trydon's core technology was taking shape, the company needed a professional front door on the web: a place that could explain the product, convince potential retail clients, let them schedule a demo, and eventually let users manage their accounts. I designed and built that website from scratch, owning every layer from design to deployment.
trydonWebsite.section2Text1: Trydon.ca is the company's main commercial surface. Beyond a standard marketing landing page, it integrates several functional systems that support the sales and onboarding process directly.
trydonWebsite.section2Bullet1: A marketing homepage presenting Trydon's value proposition, use cases, and client testimonials.
trydonWebsite.section2Bullet2: A live ROI calculator that lets prospective retail clients input their return rate and average order value to instantly see their projected savings with Trydon.
trydonWebsite.section2Bullet3: A pricing section with tiered plans and a contact form for enterprise inquiries.
trydonWebsite.section2Bullet4: An integrated meeting scheduler that lets prospects book a demo call directly from the site, without leaving to a third-party tool.
trydonWebsite.section2Bullet5: A full account management system where registered users can create their profile and manage their measurements.
trydonWebsite.section3Text1: The website is built as a single Angular application. Each section (marketing, pricing, calculator, scheduler, account) lives in its own component, keeping the initial bundle lean despite the site's breadth. The design follows a clean product-startup aesthetic — generous white space, sharp typography, and branded purple accents consistent with Trydon's identity.
trydonWebsite.section3Text2: The ROI calculator is a reactive Angular component: as the user adjusts return rate, order volume, website visits and average return costs. The projected annual savings and payback period update in real time without any server round-trip. The formula is based on industry-average return-processing costs and Trydon's internal reduction benchmarks.
trydonWebsite.section3Text3: The integrated contact form and meeting scheduler connects to a our SMTP server. When a user submits the form, the backend sends a templated email to our sales team and triggers an email sequence to the prospect with available demo slots. This keeps all scheduling within our ecosystem and allows us to track engagement directly from the website.
trydonWebsite.section3Text4: The application is deployed on AWS Lambda with a serverless architecture, keeping hosting costs proportional to actual traffic (important for an early-stage startup). The Angular app is served as a static bundle via CloudFront, while dynamic endpoints (account management, form submissions, scheduler) are handled by Lambda functions behind API Gateway.
trydonWebsite.section4Text1: I was the sole developer and designer on this project. I designed the visual identity and page structure, implemented every feature from the ROI calculator to the account system, integrated the meeting scheduler, set up the AWS infrastructure, and deployed the full application. This project gave me full-stack ownership of a live commercial product from day one.

quizGame.title: Quiz Game - Kahoot-like multiplayer platform
quizGame.description: A real-time multiplayer quiz platform with web, desktop, and mobile clients. Supports text and multiple-choice questions, AI-powered question generation, autocorrect, and full injection security hardening.
quizGame.heroSubtitle: A real-time multiplayer quiz platform across web, desktop, and mobile — with AI question generation, autocorrect, and a hardened backend.
quizGame.section1Text1: This was a third-year team software engineering project at Polytechnique Montréal. The brief was to build a full Kahoot-like experience: quiz creation, real-time multiplayer games, and support for multiple client platforms. The team delivered a web application, a cross-platform desktop client, and a Flutter mobile app, all connected to a shared NestJS backend over WebSockets.
quizGame.section2Text1: The platform lets quiz administrators create and manage quizzes with both open-text and multiple-choice questions, then host live game sessions where players join, answer in real time, and compete on a leaderboard.
quizGame.section2Bullet1: Administrators can build quizzes through a dedicated admin interface, generate questions automatically using AI. They can then build quizzes with multiple questions, point and timer for each.
quizGame.section2Bullet2: Players join game sessions from the web, desktop, or mobile client and receive questions simultaneously over a WebSocket connection, with live scoring and a results screen at the end.
quizGame.section2Bullet3: All clients share the same backend, meaning a quiz created on web can be played on mobile without any data duplication.
quizGame.section3Text1: The desktop client is an Angular web application where quiz creators can manage their question banks, build new quizzes, and launch game sessions. The player can join from this webapp or from a Flutter mobile app we built for iOS and Android. Both clients connect to the same backend and share the same WebSocket protocol for real-time game interactions.
quizGame.section3Text2: I implemented two AI features on top of the OpenAI API. The first is a question generator: given a topic and a difficulty level, it produces a ready-to-use question with a correct answer and plausible distractors. The second is an player bot that can play against human players in real time, using the same question-answering capabilities to compete on the leaderboard. Both features required careful prompt engineering to ensure the generated content was relevant, accurate, and appropriately challenging.
quizGame.section3Text3: Because user input is passed both to a database and to an LLM, the application was exposed to two distinct injection vectors: classic script injection into the front end and prompt injection against the OpenAI API. I audited the entire application and implemented systematic sanitization at every input boundary, stripping HTML and script tags from user-facing fields and neutralizing adversarial instruction patterns before any text is forwarded to the model.
quizGame.section3Text4: The server was originally written as a plain Node.js application without a formal architecture. I refactored the entire backend to NestJS, introducing a structured module system, dependency injection, typed DTOs with class-validator, and a clean separation between gateway (WebSocket), service, and persistence layers. This made the codebase significantly easier to extend and test.
quizGame.section4Text1: My contributions spanned security, AI features, and backend architecture across the full project.
quizGame.section4Bullet1: Admin frontend : Built parts of the Angular admin interface, including the quiz editor and AI-assisted creation flow.
quizGame.section4Bullet2: AI question generator : Designed and implemented the OpenAI-powered question generation feature with topic and difficulty controls.
quizGame.section4Bullet3: AI bot : Implemented the real-time bot player that competes against human players using the same question-answering capabilities.
quizGame.section4Bullet4: Security hardening : Audited and secured the full application against script injection and prompt injection at every input boundary.
quizGame.section4Bullet5: Backend refactor : Migrated the entire server from plain Node.js to a structured NestJS architecture with modules, guards, pipes, and typed DTOs.

hexAI.title: Hex AI Agent - Minimax game-playing agent
hexAI.description: A Python AI agent that plays the board game Hex using a minimax search with alpha-beta pruning and a custom heuristic combining directional path length and blockade position scoring.
hexAI.heroSubtitle: A game-playing agent for the board game Hex, built around minimax search with a directional length and blockade-position heuristic.
hexAI.section1Text1: Hex is a two-player connection game played on a rhombus-shaped board of hexagonal cells. One player tries to connect the top and bottom edges; the other tries to connect the left and right edges. Despite simple rules, the game has enormous branching factor and no drawing position, which makes it a compelling benchmark for game-tree search algorithms.
hexAI.section1Text2: The goal of this project was to design and implement an AI agent capable of playing Hex competitively against other agents. I built the full agent in Python, with the search algorithm and heuristic evaluation.
hexAI.section2Text1: Unlike chess or checkers, Hex has no draws and always has a winner. This property means the first player has a theoretical winning advantage with perfect play. In practice, finding that winning path is computationally intractable for large boards, so agents must rely on strong heuristics to approximate the value of a position.
hexAI.section2Bullet1: The board was 14×14 hexagonal cells; every cell connects to its six neighbors.
hexAI.section2Bullet2: The first player to form an unbroken chain between their two edges wins.
hexAI.section2Bullet3: There are no draws — the game always produces a winner.
hexAI.section3Sub1: Search Algorithm — Minimax with Alpha-Beta Pruning
hexAI.section3Text1: The agent uses a minimax search tree with alpha-beta pruning to explore possible game continuations. Alpha-beta pruning eliminates branches that cannot influence the final decision, significantly reducing the number of nodes evaluated and allowing the agent to search deeper within a fixed time budget. Move ordering (prioritizing 'Hot Zones') amplifies the effectiveness of the pruning.
hexAI.section3Text2: Evaluating a Hex position requires estimating how close each player is to completing a winning connection. The primary component of the heuristic is directional length: the length of the shortest virtual connection path from one edge to the other, computed using a modified Dijkstra over the board graph. A shorter path means the player is closer to winning, so the score is inversely proportional to this distance.
hexAI.section3Text3: Virtual connections account for cells not yet occupied: an empty cell on a potential path is treated as passable but at a cost, while an opponent's cell is treated as impassable. This allows the heuristic to reason about future connectivity, not just the current board state.
hexAI.section3Text4: The second heuristic component rewards positions that actively block the opponent's shortest path. Cells that sit on the opponent's critical virtual-connection bridges receive a blockade bonus, incentivizing the agent to play defensively and disrupt the opponent's strategy rather than only pursuing its own connection. The two components are combined as a weighted sum, with the weights tuned empirically.
hexAI.section4Text1: This was a solo project as part of my AI coursework. I designed the full agent, built the minimax engine with alpha-beta pruning and move ordering, designed both heuristic components, and tuned the weighting parameters through playtesting against other agents.

desjardinsKPI.title: Perimeter KPI Generator - Cybersecurity internal tool
desjardinsKPI.description: Finished building and extended an internal Python tool for external perimeter surveillance at Desjardins Bank. Scaled coverage from 6 KPIs over 3,000 assets in 6 hours to 18 KPIs over 17,000 assets in 2h30.
desjardinsKPI.heroSubtitle: Extended Desjardins' internal external-perimeter KPI tool, tripling KPI coverage, scaling to 17K assets, and cutting runtime by more than half.
desjardinsKPI.section1Text1: During my cybersecurity internship at Desjardins, one of Quebec's largest financial institutions, I was embedded in the team responsible for external perimeter surveillance, monitoring the organization's publicly exposed web assets for security risks. The team operated an internal Python tool that generated KPI reports from a set of security scanning and intelligence sources.
desjardinsKPI.section1Text2: That tool had accumulated technical debt and was constrained in both coverage and performance: it could produce 12 KPIs across roughly 3,000 assets, but a full run took 6 hours. As the number of monitored assets grew and the team needed richer reporting, the tool was becoming a bottleneck. My mandate was to overhaul it.
desjardinsKPI.section2Text1: After the extension, the tool could generate 18 KPIs across 17,000 assets, nearly six times the original scope, and complete a full run in 2 hours and 30 minutes, cutting the previous runtime by more than half. This gave the security team a significantly richer and more current view of the organization's external exposure without any increase in manual effort.
desjardinsKPI.section3Text1: The original 12 KPIs covered a limited set of signals. I worked with the team to identify the 12 additional indicators that were most valuable for external perimeter monitoring given the tooling available in the environment. I then designed and implemented the collection, normalisation, and scoring logic for each new KPI, integrating with the existing security tooling and APIs already in use by the team. The names and specific capabilities of those tools are confidential under the internship NDA.
desjardinsKPI.section3Text2: The original tool processed assets certificate analysis sequentially, which was untenable with thousands of assets. I refactored the processing pipeline to work concurrently, introducing batching and caching along with the introduction of a new tool unused for this part of the script yet. I also addressed memory usage patterns that caused degradation on large runs, enabling the tool to handle the full 17,000-asset scope reliably.
desjardinsKPI.section3Text3: Beyond parallelism, performance gains came from eliminating redundant API calls by caching intermediate results across KPI computations that shared upstream data sources, and from restructuring the execution graph so independent KPI collection pipelines ran simultaneously. The combined effect reduced the wall-clock time from 6 hours to 2h30 despite the much larger asset count and broader KPI set.
desjardinsKPI.section4Text1: This was an individual internship project, as i was modifying the team made tool, i was guided by my team. I audited the existing codebase, designed the new architecture, refactored the processing pipeline for concurrency and scale, and validated the output against the team's reporting requirements. Working on internal tooling for a major financial institution's security operations gave me direct exposure to real-world threat intelligence workflows and the engineering constraints of operating at that scale.

solarNav.title: Solar Navigation - Interplanetary route planner
solarNav.description: A real-time solar system navigation system that uses NASA ephemeris data to compute fuel-optimal interplanetary trajectories, leveraging gravity assists as slingshot maneuvers. Features a React dashboard and a live Unity 3D journey visualizer.
solarNav.heroSubtitle: A real-time interplanetary route planner that pulls live NASA data, computes gravity-assist trajectories, and renders your journey in a dynamic 3D solar system.
solarNav.section1Text1: PolyHX 2024 is Polytechnique Montréal's annual hackathon. Our team chose the Space Challenge, which tasked participants with building a project connected to space exploration. We arrived second in our category with a system capable of planning real fuel-optimal routes between planets using live orbital data. The challenge pushed us to think at the intersection of orbital mechanics, graph theory, and interactive visualization — all within a hackathon time constraint.
solarNav.section2Text1: The application lets a user choose a departure planet and a destination planet, then computes the most fuel-efficient route through the solar system including intermediate planetary flybys that use gravity assists to gain speed without burning extra fuel.
solarNav.section2Bullet1: Planet positions are fetched in real time from the NASA Horizons API, giving trajectories grounded in actual orbital mechanics rather than approximations.
solarNav.section2Bullet2: The path-finding algorithm identifies gravity-assist opportunities: routes that swing past an intermediate planet to gain momentum and reduce the total delta-v cost of the journey.
solarNav.section2Bullet3: A React dashboard shows the computed route, fuel estimates, and travel duration across selectable planet pairs.
solarNav.section2Bullet4: A Unity visualizer renders the full journey in a live 3D solar system, animating the spacecraft's path and showing each planetary encounter in real time.
solarNav.section3Text1: The route-finding problem is modeled as a graph search over a space-time graph: nodes are (planet, time) pairs and edge weights represent the delta-v cost of a Hohmann-like transfer between two planets at those times. Gravity assists are encoded as negative-cost bonuses on edges that route through a third planet. A modified Dijkstra search finds the minimum delta-v path from origin to destination, exploring departures across a time window to find the optimal launch date.
solarNav.section3Text2: Planet positions and velocities are queried from the NASA Horizons System API at the time steps required by the graph construction. This means the routes reflect the actual positions of the planets on the requested travel dates, not static or averaged orbital elements.
solarNav.section3Text3: The Unity visualizer receives the computed trajectory as a JSON payload from the Python backend and renders it over a dynamic solar system scene. Each planet is animated along its real orbital path using the same ephemeris data, and the spacecraft follows the computed trajectory with smooth interpolation between waypoints.
solarNav.section3Text4: The scene includes a preview mode that shows a time-lapse of the full journey (from departure to arrival) so the user can see the planets moving in real time as the spacecraft travels between them. Camera controls let the viewer follow the spacecraft or observe the system from above.
solarNav.section4Text1: My contributions were split between the algorithm design phase at the start of the hackathon and full ownership of the Unity visualization.
solarNav.section4Bullet1: Algorithm design : Participated in the architectural brainstorm that produced the space-time graph formulation and the gravity-assist encoding strategy for the modified Dijkstra.
solarNav.section4Bullet2: Unity visualization : Built the entire 3D journey viewer: solar system scene, orbital animations driven by NASA data, spacecraft path rendering, time-lapse preview, and the JSON interface to the Python backend.

escapeEngine.title: Escape The Engine - 2D platformer with losing mechanics
escapeEngine.description: A 2D Unity platformer made for CreativeJam 2024 in a team of 7, built around the theme 'losing mechanics'. After each of the 5 levels, the player permanently loses a core ability — direction, jump, vision, and more. Set on a train.
escapeEngine.heroSubtitle: A 2D train-set platformer where each completed level strips away one of the player's core abilities — until almost nothing is left.
escapeEngine.section1Text1: CreativeJam 2024 was a game jam where teams were given the theme 'losing mechanics' and a fixed time window to build a complete, playable game. Our team of 7 interpreted the theme literally: a platformer where the player does not just face increasingly hard levels, but permanently loses one gameplay mechanic after completing each one.
escapeEngine.section1Text2: The game is set on a moving train, with each level taking place in a different train car. As the player escapes toward the engine, they leave behind an ability they relied on to get there, making the journey both narratively and mechanically about loss.
escapeEngine.section2Text1: Escape The Engine is a 2D platformer with 5 levels. Each level is designed to teach the player to rely on a specific mechanic and then take it away at the end. The mechanic loss is permanent for the rest of the run, forcing the player to adapt their entire playstyle as the game progresses.
escapeEngine.section3Text1: The player controller is built as a capability-flag system: every core ability (horizontal movement, jumping, vision, time control) is a boolean flag on the controller that can be disabled at runtime. When the player completes a level and a mechanic is stripped, the corresponding flag is turned off and the UI reflects the loss immediately. This architecture made it straightforward for the team to design levels around specific capability sets without duplicating movement code.
escapeEngine.section3Text2: As the project builder, I was responsible for assembling all team members' contributions into the final executable. This meant managing Unity scene integration, resolving asset conflicts, configuring the build pipeline for Windows, and producing the final distributable .exe delivered at the end of the jam. I also built and owned the movement framework that all level designers on the team built their puzzles against.
escapeEngine.section4Text1: Within the 7-person team, my responsibilities covered the foundational systems that the rest of the game was built on top of.
escapeEngine.section4Bullet1: Player movement framework — Designed and implemented the capability-flag architecture for the player controller, used by every level in the game.
escapeEngine.section4Bullet2: Interaction system — Built the player interaction system handling collectables, triggers, and mechanic-loss events at level boundaries.
escapeEngine.section4Bullet3: Project build — Assembled all team contributions, managed the Unity project structure, and produced the final distributable .exe for submission.

homunculus.title: Homunculus - Neural acquisition & stimulation software
homunculus.description: Contributed to the desktop software for Homunculus, a company building hardware for MEA neural acquisition and stimulation. The app is built with Rust and Tauri for the hardware layer and React for the interface. I improved the frontend, added multichannel matrix visualization, and built the auto-connect system over UDP and serial.
homunculus.heroSubtitle: Desktop software for MEA neural acquisition and stimulation - built with Rust and React for hardware reliability using Tauri
homunculus.section1Text1: Homunculus is a company developing hardware for Micro-Electrode Array (MEA) neural acquisition and stimulation. MEA systems record and stimulate electrical activity across many electrodes simultaneously, enabling researchers to study neural circuits at fine spatial resolution. The company needed a robust desktop application capable of interfacing directly with their hardware to control stimulation parameters, acquire signals in real time, and visualize multichannel data.
homunculus.section1Text2: I contributed to the development of this software, working primarily on the frontend layer and the device connection system.
homunculus.section2Text1: The application connects to the Homunculus MEA hardware device and provides a full interface for researchers to configure, record, and visualize neural activity across all acquisition channels simultaneously.
homunculus.section2Bullet1: Connects to the MEA hardware over USB serial and ethernet, with automatic device discovery removing the need for manual configuration.
homunculus.section2Bullet2: Streams multichannel acquisition data in real time, displaying all active electrode channels simultaneously in a synchronized matrix view.
homunculus.section2Bullet3: Allows researchers to configure stimulation parameters and trigger stimulation events directly from the interface.
homunculus.section2Bullet4: Provides a tool to configure closed loop algorithms that react to incoming neural signals with custom stimulation patterns.
homunculus.section3Text1: The application is built with Tauri, which wraps a Rust backend in a desktop shell with a React frontend. The hardware-critical path ( serial communication, data streaming, and stimulation control ) lives entirely in Rust, where the memory model and deterministic performance are essential for reliable low-latency acquisition. The React frontend communicates with the Rust layer through Tauri's typed command and event bridge.
homunculus.section3Text2: Device connection was previously manual, requiring the user to identify the correct serial port or IP address each time. I built an automatic discovery system that first listens for a UDP broadcast from the MEA device on the local network and attempts a TCP connection if one is found. If no UDP broadcast is detected, it falls back to enumerating available serial ports and probing each one for the device's identification signature. This reduced connection friction for researchers to a single button press.
homunculus.section3Text3: MEA systems record from many electrodes simultaneously, and researchers need to see all channels at once to identify spatial patterns of activity. I designed and implemented a multichannel matrix visualization in React: a grid layout where each cell corresponds to one electrode, displaying a live waveform trace. The matrix scales to the electrode count of the connected device. Rendering performance was carefully managed to keep all channels updating smoothly at the acquisition sampling rate.
homunculus.section4Text1: I joined the project as a contributor focused on the user-facing and connectivity layers.
homunculus.section4Bullet1: Auto-connect: Designed and implemented the full UDP discovery and serial port probing system in Rust, exposing it to the frontend through a Tauri command.
homunculus.section4Bullet2: Multichannel matrix view: Built the React multichannel visualization component with live waveform rendering across all acquisition electrodes.
homunculus.section4Bullet3: Frontend improvements: Contributed broader improvements to the React interface, refining layout and transforming the user experience from a on page of controls to a adaptble dashboard with preset configurations.
homunculus.section5Text1: ⚠️ As Homunculus technology is protected, the software is not publicly available yet. For inquiries about the software or the project, please contact me directly through the contact form.

gameNight.title: GameNight - Party tools mobile app
gameNight.description: A cross-platform iOS and Android mobile app built with Expo and React Native. GameNight gives friend groups a toolkit for game nights — team generators, timers, dice, and more — with the ability to compose custom games from tools and host multiplayer sessions over WebSocket.
gameNight.heroSubtitle: A mobile toolkit for game nights - compose your own games from party tools, host multiplayer sessions, and play with friends on iOS or Android.
gameNight.section1Text1: Game nights often involve switching between multiple apps for timers, team generation, scoreboards, and dice. GameNight was designed to consolidate all those tools into one cohesive, multiplayer-aware mobile experience. The key idea is composability: instead of a fixed set of mini-games, users build their own by combining primitive tools into a custom game flow, and then host that game live for their friends over WebSocket.
gameNight.section2Text1: GameNight is a party toolkit app where every feature is a standalone composable tool. Users can use tools individually or chain them into a custom game, then host that game in a multiplayer room where all participants stay in sync.
gameNight.section2Bullet1: A library of party tools (dice, timers, team generators, randomizers, scorekeepers and more) each usable standalone or as part of a custom game.
gameNight.section2Bullet2: Custom game builder: users compose an ordered sequence of tools with their chosen configurations to create a reusable game format they can save and replay.
gameNight.section2Bullet3: Multiplayer hosting: the game host's NestJS backend broadcasts game state over WebSocket to all connected players, keeping every device in sync without any additional setup.
gameNight.section2Bullet4: Account and subscription system: users can create an account, manage their profile, and access premium tools through a subscription tier.
gameNight.section3Text1: The app is built with Expo and React Native, targeting both iOS and Android from a single codebase. The architecture is highly componentized by design: every party tool is an isolated, self-contained React Native component with a standard interface, making it straightforward to add new tools without touching the rest of the app. The custom game builder composes these tool components dynamically at runtime based on the user's saved game configuration.
gameNight.section3Text2: Multiplayer sessions are powered by a NestJS backend using WebSocket gateways. When a user hosts a game, a room is created on the server and all joining players subscribe to that room's event stream. Every game action (a dice roll, a timer start, a team shuffle) is sent to the server as an event, which applies the action to the authoritative game state and broadcasts the result to all connected clients. This keeps every player's screen in sync regardless of network timing.
gameNight.section3Text3: The core architecture, multiplayer system, account and subscription system, and frontend are complete. The remaining work is expanding the tool library - adding more party tools to fill out the game creation palette. Once the tool library reaches a satisfying depth, the app will be submitted to the App Store and Google Play.
gameNight.section4Text1: This is a solo personal project. I designed and built every part of it: the mobile app architecture, the component system, the custom game builder, the multiplayer backend, the account and subscription system, and the full frontend. The project is in its final stretch before store submission.
gameNight.section5Text1: ⚠️ The app is still in development and not publicly available yet. For inquiries about the software or the project, please contact me directly through the contact form.

hifi.hero: A self-hosted, WiFi-synchronized multiroom audio system built from scratch with Raspberry Pi hardware and a custom React control interface.
hifi.section1Text1: I built a fully self-hosted multiroom audio system from scratch using Raspberry Pi hardware. The goal was to have synchronized music playing across multiple rooms, controllable from a custom touchscreen dashboard, with Spotify integration and vinyl input support without relying on any subscription-based multiroom platform.
hifi.section1Bullet1: Raspberry Pi 4 as the central server with a 7" touchscreen
hifi.section1Bullet2: Raspberry Pi Zero 2W clients connected to speakers via AUX
hifi.section1Bullet3: Bluetooth speaker support with per-room volume control
hifi.section1Bullet4: Spotify Connect + Spotify Web API for full playback control
hifi.section2Text1: The server runs Snapcast, which handles millisecond-precise audio synchronization across all clients over WiFi. Librespot (the open-source Spotify client) is embedded directly into Snapserver as a native source, eliminating the pipe timing issues that come from running it as a separate process.
hifi.section2Text2: Each client (whether a Zero 2W connected via USB audio adapter or a Bluetooth speaker running a dedicated Snapclient service) receives the same audio stream and plays it in perfect sync. ALSA's plughw interface handles sample rate conversion transparently on each device.
hifi.section2Text3: The audio chain is entirely local: Spotify streams to librespot on the Pi, Snapserver encodes and distributes it over the LAN, and each Snapclient decodes and outputs it. No cloud dependency, no latency from external services.
hifi.section3Text1: The most significant challenge was clock synchronization. Snapcast uses the system's monotonic clock (which starts at boot) to timestamp audio chunks. When two devices boot at different times, the difference in their monotonic clocks causes Snapcast to discard chunks as "too old" or "too far in the future", resulting in total silence on the client.
hifi.section3Text2: The fix involved two layers: using Chrony to synchronize wall-clock time via NTP across both devices, and compensating for the remaining monotonic offset using Snapcast's built-in latency parameter. A clean simultaneous reboot of both devices eliminates the offset entirely.
hifi.section3Text3: Another challenge was librespot's fixed sample rate output. Despite the Snapserver config specifying 48000Hz, librespot always outputs at 44100Hz. Rather than fighting it, the solution was to align the entire pipeline to 44100Hz and use plughw on clients to let ALSA handle any device-level conversion.
hifi.section3Text4: Getting Spotify OAuth PKCE to work over plain HTTP on a local network required a pure JavaScript SHA-256 implementation, since the Web Crypto API's crypto.subtle is blocked on non-HTTPS origins. The solution was to add a self-signed SSL certificate via OpenSSL and proxy the Snapcast WebSocket through nginx with WSS support.
hifi.section4Text1: The touchscreen interface is a React + Vite app served by nginx over HTTPS, designed specifically for touch input on a 7" display. It communicates with two real-time APIs simultaneously:
hifi.section4Bullet1: Snapcast JSON-RPC over WebSocket : per-room volume, mute, source switching
hifi.section4Bullet2: Spotify Web API : now playing, search, playlist browsing, playback control
hifi.section4Bullet3: Spotify PKCE OAuth : no backend required, auth runs entirely in the browser
hifi.section4Bullet4: Auto-reconnecting WebSocket : survives server restarts transparently
hifi.section4Text2: The app uses Zustand for state management and polls the Spotify API every 3 seconds for now-playing updates. Room cards update optimistically on volume changes for instant UI feedback, with the WebSocket confirming the actual state.
hifi.section5Text1: Bluetooth speakers are integrated as first-class rooms in the system. Each paired speaker runs its own dedicated Snapclient systemd service with a unique hostID, routing audio through PulseAudio's Bluetooth sink. This makes them appear automatically as separate rooms in the dashboard with individual volume sliders.
hifi.section5Text2: Bluetooth introduces ~100-200ms of inherent latency. This is compensated per-service using Snapcast's --latency parameter, keeping all rooms audibly synchronized regardless of the output medium.
hifi.section6Text1: This project was a deep dive into Linux audio, networking, and embedded systems far more complex than expected at the start. Key takeaways:
hifi.section6Bullet1: Linux audio stack (ALSA, PulseAudio, PipeWire) is powerful but fragile - small mismatches in sample rates or buffer sizes cause silent failures
hifi.section6Bullet2: Monotonic vs wall-clock time is a real distributed systems problem, even at home-lab scale
hifi.section6Bullet3: Browser security constraints (HTTPS, crypto.subtle, mixed content) apply even on local networks and require real engineering solutions
hifi.section6Bullet4: Systemd is the right tool for managing multiple interdependent audio services - restart policies and dependency ordering matter a lot

summary.part1: Final-year Software Engineering student at Polytechnique Montréal.
summary.part2: Passionate about building impactful software with experience in web, cybersecurity, video games, and robotics.
summary.part3: Proven leadership as a co-founder, project manager, and university team president.
summary.part4: Seeking a software engineering graduate job in Big Tech with strong skills in programming, system design, and teamwork.
